speaker: interviewer
text: --15, 1995. Interview with Sol Urbach, tape number 4. You were talking about being in the barracks at Gross Rosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So our life in Gross Rosen was a-- was very much a mystery to us and how long-- what from here-- whether we will ever get out of this place. But lo and behold, sometimes after three or four days-- and again, the days were-- night and day were running into one another, and nobody kept counting on-- on what day it is. One of those evenings, as we were sitting there in the laps of one another on the floor of the barrack, a SS guard came into the room with a list in his hand. And he began to read names. Now, it-- you didn't have to be a scientist, even at that time, to recognize quickly that being on any kind of a list is better than remaining there in Gross Rosen. So myself, remembering from what I went through at that time, that night, I became extremely anxious as to whether they will have me on the list or not. I-- I knew that they were calling names. But who they are going to call and who they are not going to call, I had no idea. So my anxiety drove me nuts because I also began to think that my name-- my name being-- starting with a U would-- would take so long before they would get to me that maybe this is going to hurt me eventually that they are not going to get me on that list. And I-- I went through hell. I was sweating. I was in fear. And it didn't even dawn on me at that time that they did not call in alphabetical order. I somehow build it up in my mind that this is in alphabetical order, and therefore, my name, starting with a U, will be at the tail end. And I was extremely anxious throughout the night. As it happens, they called me, but not as a very end where the U's would be. But they did call me someplace. But the shocking thing is that at that time, when I was so anxious and did not-- did not know fully what was going on, I did not even realize that they called another-- another Urbach by the same name. And this is not 100% verified, but nevertheless, it appears that way. They-- they did call another Urbach by the name of David, and David Urbach would have been-- could have been-- my father. But my anxiety and my fear of that night did not permit me to hear that they called another name by that-- another Urbach. So I knew nothing of another Urbach winding up in our group at all. And I never met this fellow, David Urbach, at all. And I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that that name was called?

speaker: interviewee
text: See-- well, he appears now on Schindler's list. And so there are only two possibilities how-- how people arrived there. One would have been by way of Gross Rosen, which would have made it likely that maybe my father survived, that maybe it was a different David Urbach. We will never know that. But this was the-- one of the two possibilities is that somebody came by way of Krakau- Plaszow to Gross Rosen. And the other possibility would have been that sometime later on, while we were in Brunnlitz and some of the lost railroad cars that Schindler brought into his own camp-- that maybe there was a David Urbach that came, and it was that transport. But it somehow has a ring of possibility that this Urbach arrived together with us. But nevertheless, I did not know. Anxiety and fear combined and the lack of rationality by then-- I had no idea that another Urbach was called. After all these names were called-- and the best I can figure out, there were 700 people-- we were loaded on cattle cars, and Gross Rosen again, and shipped out from Gross Rosen. And again, I-- I have to stress that I had no idea what this list is all about. Nobody told us at that time that this is a list that Oskar Schindler has created and that this is a list that will take us to Oskar Schindler's factory. It was just not known to me at the time. But nevertheless, we were shipped out of this concentration camp, and there was some joy in the fact that we were leaving this concentration camp. What the future held for us, we didn't know. But nevertheless, we were happy that we were leaving this camp. Unbeknownst to us, Oskar Schindler did pull off a scheme and has received a linen factory that the Germans gave to him in the town of Brunnlitz. And he promises the German authorities that he will convert this linen factory into a pots and pans and into a-- something that makes cartridges for the rockets, and this will be a vital industry. And vital in all this is that work force that is so highly skilled that now is trapped in Gross Rosen and is also trapped in Auschwitz. The women are trapped in Auschwitz. And evidently, he pulled off the-- the whole scheme that in Gross Rosen they carefully called our names and loaded us on our-- on the railroad-- in the cattle cars. And as the movie shows, the women were similarly loaded on cattle cars and shipped out of Auschwitz. Also, at times-- at that time during the war, I thought that the ladies out of Auschwitz were walking on foot towards our camp. But whether they walked part of the trip and part of the trip they were on the railroad, I don't know at this point anymore unless I would verify it with somebody that was in that group with the ladies. We did, after a-- after a few days on sidings again being shipped-- being shifted from one track to another, we arrived in-- still in October of 1944, we arrived at this town of Brunnlitz, where Oskar Schindler was-- was awaiting us. He was in the camp already. He had created some accommodations for us. He had barbed wire surrounding the premises. If I recollected correctly, the barbed wires were no longer the same fearful barbed wires that we had in the other concentration camps. They did not seem to have the watchtowers, the fearful watchtowers that were in the other concentration camps. But nevertheless, there were some barbed wires surrounding this linen-- this old linen factory, and we began work at that time in that factory. Schindler began to build his offices. I was again employed as a cabinet maker-carpenter and worked with a bunch of people that were doing carpentry like myself. In this camp, life was much more bearable, and we began to sense-- in a short while, we began to sense that maybe we do have a chance to survive the war in this concentration camp. It was somehow in the air. With Schindler being there and with the SS guards not entering too much of the camp, we began to think that maybe something is changing. Maybe we-- we do have a chance. I was taken-- on many days, I was taken out of this concentration camp together with another fellow by the name of Blazenstein, who now lives in Israel-- or the last I heard of lived in Israel. [INAUDIBLE] The two of us would be marched out of this camp in Brunnlitz and taken to his villa. The villa was located in a mill, inside a mill behind the water. We were separated from the road by a-- by a canal of water. And in that villa, the two of us were supposed to fix-- repair his-- Schindler-- Oskar Schindler's furniture, maintain it cleaned. I have no idea whether Oskar Schindler ever stayed there or not. I don't remember seeing Mr. Schindler or Oskar Schindler at that villa. On the other hand, I remember them in the--. In the factory itself, there were accommodations where Oskar Schindler stayed. Off the factory hall, there were some rooms that they were living in. On many occasions, I was able to jump out of the villa window. And the reason I could do that because in those days, in 1945 late into the war, the SS guards were no longer those spic-and-span young devils, the young killers of SS, but they were the older people now serving in the-- in the army and not being on the front. They were guarding us. So the guard that would bring us into this villa would occasionally fall asleep. As he was sitting on the-- on the sofa bed, he was falling asleep and slept while we were working or cleaning. And we even had a chance occasionally to make believe that we are working on the radio, repairing the radio, or messing around near the radio. And we would occasionally hear the news from BBC in Polish. So we had an idea that maybe the war is turning against the Germans, and we dared hoping at that time. Also, while I was-- while the guard would be sleeping at that time, when I jumped out of the window to the mill, I would go into the mill. And the Czechoslovak workers working in the mill were extremely friendly people, and they would give me anything I wanted. Whether I wanted oatmeal or grits or-- or some other products that they were making in that mill, they would allow me to take that stuff and take it with me. I would fill up my-- tie my pants at the bottom and-- and dump some stuff into them and also fill my toolbox. And I was able to bring it into the-- into the concentration camp and divide it out to-- to people that were in-- in need, in desperate need, being sick or something. I would divide it to different people.

speaker: interviewer
text: When that guard fell asleep, did you ever think of taking his gun and running away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was not a chance, really, to run anyway. We had absolutely no idea where we were. I, certainly, as a youngster, was-- school finishing for me some five years ago, I had no idea where we were. I had no idea where you would go. I had no idea whether there is any friend outside of there, and it really was like we were locked in by our own abilities by-- to escape. And it was not even on the minds of other people. There was no one escaping at that time. We-- after a while, if you're doomed, you begin to feel like doomed. And we remained in place being doomed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, it's a good question. Right now, I would be asking the same question. Why didn't I take the gun and go off? But, no, that was not on our mind. We would come in. Every night, we would be led by the same SS guard to the camp and would come into the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: On one of those occasions, when we came into the camp, the-- the women lived separated. The 300 women lived practically in the same building, but they were separated by a masonry wall. And the entrance to the women's barracks was by a separate staircase. And-- and that staircase would have been surrounded by barbed wire so men could not get into those quarters. But in one of those-- one of those days when we returned from the mill, I myself, together with this fellow Blazenstein, we came into the mill, and we had some food with us, some food to distribute. And at that time, as we came in, we noticed that there is no guard in front of the ladies' barrack. Normally, there would have been a-- a gate, either closed or-- or a lady SS guard. But in this situation, when we arrived there, there was nobody there. So we thought this is a great time to sneak in. And we were daredevils by that time because I don't think we would have done it two years ago. But by that time, we dared. And we-- lo and behold, we got into the ladies' quarters. There were no ladies there that I recall. But we dropped off some of that food stuff. We simply dropped that off, and we went off. But as we came out, there was a-- a woman SS guard standing at the-- at the same gate that was supposed to be before. And so now we were dying 100 deaths on what could happen to us. But again, this was Schindler's camp, and the situation was not similar to other concentration camps, and nothing happened immediately. But at the daily assembly-- at the Appelplatz-- and the assemblies there took place inside the factory hall, in a hall. At that-- in that evening, a-- the commandant or the SS commandant of that camp came forth and yelled out. And I don't speak German, but certain things that were said at that time left such deep impressions that I cannot forget them. And this commandant yelled out in German, [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't say so many German words in my life as I all of a sudden remember. But that's exactly what he said. And what this means is, where are the two men that were in the ladies' barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: And for a while, as it happens, the two of us who worked together as carpenters stood together. And for a while, we hesitated. But again, this is 1945, Schindler's camp. And we hesitated for a while, but then we decided that we better step forward and-- and tell them who it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we step up and tell, we-- we're the one. We were in the [GERMAN]. And all the commandant wanted to know is-- is which SS guard was there. And we had absolutely no trouble at pointing at one of the SS guards. Whether she was the one or not didn't make any difference to us. We simply pointed to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it tells a little bit about the time and place and that we did have a chance even with a lie-- was a-- was a violation of rules and all this because all this commandant did is chew out this SS guard-- this SS lady guard rather than hurt us at all. We were put back into the formation of the rest of the inmates, and nothing ever happened to us. So it was one of those incidents. Another incident I remember in this camp is when-- and at that time, the rumor mill was working tremendously. Whatever was happening to Schindler-- to Schindler was very important news to us because we somehow knew that if anything happens to Schindler, we are in danger, that this was already like, you know, above board. We already knew it, that Schindler is our keeper, that--. And rumor had it one day that Schindler was called to the SS headquarters in-- in some place not far away in the city near where we were to respond to a charge that we had more food in this camp than the populations, of the Czechoslovak population outside of this camp. And the-- the way I remember that event is that we were locked in-- in the-- we were not locked in, but we were in the barracks. And we were standing practically by the doorways and windows waiting, hoping that Schindler is going to return. We knew that he went, and we somehow-- either he told somebody and somebody told somebody else, and before you know the whole camp knew it, that Schindler was called into headquarters to answer charges. We were standing by the windows, doors, awaiting Schindler, our protector. That's the only one that can protect us. And lo and behold, sometimes late in the day, Oskar Schindler arrives. And if I understood it, when he loaded up-- when he went there, he went there with a load of whiskey and chocolates and everything in his car that he took off to the headquarters. But when he came back, he was drunk. I remember seeing him in drunken state, getting out of that car of his and calling for the engineer. And if I recall it correctly, the engineer's name-- a German engineer, not a-- not a Polish or a Jewish inmate, but a German engineer by the name of-- of [? Sherbourne ?] or [? Shelbourne ?]. He called him out. And in German, we could-- now, I couldn't hear the details or anything. But he was pointing out to him and yelling that over here on this side, we will have to build a-- a tall-- at that time, I understood it somehow to be 30-foot tall fence, solid so that people couldn't see to the inside of the camp. Now, those-- those things I remember by myself seeing and happening in a clear mind already because this was towards the end of the war. And we possibly even began to think, which did not happen in-- up to this point, really. And so our-- our living there-- during the time that we were there, some stray railroad cars would arrive in there, and frozen people would be found in the railroad cars. Mr. Schindler had a great deal to do with that by-- by helping, her physically helping the inmates to unload those people. Some of them in the railroad cars were frozen, were dead, were horribly injured. Some of the skin-- on some of those that were still alive, some of the skin was froze to the wooden floors or walls of the railroad cars. This was winter time. But some of them remained alive and were brought back to health in our camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you participate in the unloading?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- on some occasion, I did participate if I wasn't away, if I was in the camp. Everybody had a hand in going out to help with the unloading. And rumor would have it at that time that Schindler was not producing anything. And not only was this a rumor, but from my own experience, I also knew it, that we are not producing anything. We are not making anything. We're building offices. We are making cabinets. We are maintaining blackout shades. We are doing many things, but there is no productivity as such. There was inside this great factory hall, there was a surrounded area by wire. It was separated from the rest of the hall from where I work. And inside that wired-off area were the people that were working on the lathe and producing some samples and producing some machinery, preparing machinery like if the war was going to last forever. And they were planning and scheming some new equipment, new machinery, and working on that. And this was on the inside of the big hall. The rest of us worked on that big hall. At one time, I remember winding up on a-- in the infirmary, sick. And I was extremely happy that I wound up because all of a sudden, the conditions there were a little better than what I had in my barracks. And I-- I remember also winding up there together with a friend of mine who I believe had pneumonia at that time. He is a dentist from Israel by Olek Allerhand, and he's now living in Israel. So life in that camp was bearable on-- on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get medication when you were sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody needed medication to enough. I don't recall getting any medication, just a rest or some hot water or hot soup. I don't recall any medications at all. There is a chance that there were some for some other extreme cases. I-- I was not one of those. I was sick, and I don't even recall what my sickness was, but I wound up in this infirmary for a short time. And I remember being next to a friend of mine, which made me extremely happy that we were together there. We were both 17 years old at this time. And this life inside the small concentration camp in Brunnlitz went on until May 7, 1945. May 7, 1945, Oskar Schindler assembled us in the-- in that same hall of the factory, and he told that tomorrow, May 8, 1945, the German armies will surrender to the-- to the British, French, and American, Russian armies. And the war will be over, and we will be free people. At the same time, he says that he already arranged to-- that the SS guards are gone from the camp. And he also told us that in his office-- and he pointed to his office to-- in that-- in those quarters that I explained before where he was living-- that in those quarters, on the desks, there are 30 revolvers with ammunition. And he told us that those of us that know how to use those revolvers should get a hold of them and protect the rest of the inmates from some stray German units that wouldn't know that the war came to an end and would still try to harm us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- he explained that, and he also told us at that time that the warehouses are full of-- of different materials, and we should put some people in charge of it. And he explained it in just those words. Some of us should take charge of those and divide it equally amongst the inmates, that everybody should get his share of what is in the warehouses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he at that same time told us that he will have to escape from here-- and by that time, we had a pretty good idea why-- that he would have to escape from here, and he would need some help from our inmates to see him across the boundaries of Czechoslovakia into Germany.
